title: Hunt the Switch — Other Side, Same Read
game_context: 5-on-5 half-court. A high screen happened a beat before the scene opened — the defense switched, and a slower / smaller defender (x4) is now matched on you on the LEFT wing. The original on-ball defender (x1) has cleared to the screener. You are catching the ball with the mismatch already established. Same situation as HUNT-01, mirrored to the left wing.
possession_setup: You are on the left wing in triple-threat. x4, the post defender, picked you up after the switch. He is square but his feet are heavier than yours and his hips are not yet under him — the matchup is real. The other three defenders are in shell positions and not yet stunting. No tag is pre-positioned at the rim.
decision_moment: You feel the mismatch on the catch. Beat one freezes on the matchup — see who is on you and how he is standing. The play unfreezes for one dribble of probe. Beat two freezes on his recovery — the side he slid to, and the foot he is leaving behind.
visible_cue: Beat 1: x4 is on your hip, chest square, but he is the post defender — not your size. Beat 2: after your probe his outside foot has dropped toward the middle; his baseline foot is trailing. The lane along the LEFT baseline is open before help can rotate.
prompt: Same switch, other wing. Beat 1 — see the mismatch. Beat 2 — read his recovery. What's the read?

choices.label: Drive baseline against his recovery foot.
choices.feedback_text: Best read. The mismatch is yours and his trailing foot is on the baseline side. Same chain, other side — you took it.

choices.label: Pull up off the dribble — he bit on the probe.
choices.feedback_text: Okay. If he commits forward you can rise, but the baseline drive against the recovery is the cleaner exploit on this mismatch.
choices.partial_feedback_text: Pulling up trades a rim attempt for a contested two. Drive the trailing foot — same offer the defense made on the right side.

choices.label: Pass it back to the point guard and reset.
choices.feedback_text: Not yet. Resetting gives up the mismatch. The defense forced this matchup on themselves — make them live with it on either side of the floor.

choices.label: Drive at the help defender on the weak-side corner.
choices.feedback_text: Not yet. HUNT attacks the mismatch, not the help. The cue you need is on your defender's feet, not on the corner.

best_read: Drive the LEFT baseline. The switch handed you a slower defender, and his probe recovery left his back foot trailing on the baseline side. Attack that lane before help can rotate over from the strong side. Same read as HUNT-01 — the side just changed.
acceptable_reads: Pull up if he bites forward on the dribble — keeps the possession alive but trades a rim attempt for a contested mid-range, so it is worse EV than the baseline drive.
bad_reads: Pass back and reset — gives up the mismatch the defense just handed you.
Attack the corner help — HUNT is about exploiting the new matchup, not chasing rotated help.
common_miss_reason: Treating each side of the floor as a new puzzle. Players who learned HUNT-01 on the right wing sometimes hesitate on the left because the geometry feels unfamiliar — but the cue grammar is identical, only mirrored. Hesitation lets x4 reset his feet.
why_best_read_works: A switch is a defensive concession on either side of the floor. The cost is a slower defender on a quicker offensive player. When he probes his recovery, his weight commits to one side — the side his feet did not cover is the side you attack. Mirroring the scene flips left↔right but not the underlying read: the trailing foot still points toward the side that just opened up, and the open lane is still the side his foot is leaving behind.
decoder_teaching_point: HUNT is two reads in a row, on either side of the floor. Beat 1: see the mismatch. Beat 2: read the recovery. The side doesn't change the cue — it only changes which baseline you're driving.

feedback.correct: Good chain. Same read, other side. The mismatch + the recovery foot worked the same way on the left wing.
feedback.partial: Right idea, second-best exploit. The mismatch was bigger than a pull-up — drive the trailing foot next time.
feedback.wrong: Look at the chain. Beat 1 named the mismatch; beat 2 named the recovery. Mirror does not change the cue.

self_review_checklist: Did I wait for beat 2 before I committed, the same as on the right side?
Did I track his feet during the inter-beat probe, not the side of the floor?
Did I attack the baseline his recovery foot was leaving open?
Did I trust that the cue grammar mirrors cleanly when the floor flips?

coach_validation.notes: Pack 2 (Phase δ-A mirror) — HUNT-01 left-wing mirror variant. Same chained-read shape as HUNT-01, mirrored across the y-axis. Mirror variants validate that mastery of HUNT D1 transfers across the floor; if recognition collapses on the mirror the player has memorized geometry, not the chain. coach_validation.level=medium per LINT-HUNT-05 (matches HUNT-01).

source_research_basis: Mirror variants are the canonical post-mastery exposure pattern (see BDW-T1-02). Same source taxonomy as HUNT-01: pre-set switch mismatch as the entry-level chained-read shape.
explanation_md: **Hunt the Advantage — Pre-Set Switch, Other Wing.** Same chained read as HUNT-01, flipped to the left side of the floor. The defense switched a beat before the scene started, and a slower defender is on you. Beat one shows you the matchup — observe, do not commit. The play unfreezes and you probe with one dribble; the defender slides, and his recovery leaves one foot trailing. Beat two freezes that recovery. The trailing foot points away from the rim — drive baseline against it. **The side does not change the chain.** If you read it on the right, you read it on the left.

wrongDemos.caption: You pulled up. The shot was contested before the mismatch could matter.
You reset to the guard. The switch held and the mismatch was wasted.
You drove at the corner help. They rotated and stripped it.
